channel.title: cascivo blog
channel.description: Notes on building cascivo: the AI layer, signal-driven reactivity, modern CSS, and what shipping an owned-code design system actually looks like.

item.title: Giving your AI coding agent a component library it can’t hallucinate
item.description: AI agents write plausible-looking UI code that invents props, colors, and class names that don’t exist. cascivo ships a machine-readable manifest per component so agents can select, scaffold, and validate their own output instead of guessing.

item.title: Signals vs. useState: what actually changes
item.description: cascivo components use signals instead of useState — not as a style preference, but because it changes what re-renders. Here’s what that means in practice, and where the line for "genuine internal state" actually sits.

item.title: Owned-code components: what shadcn got right, and where we took it further
item.description: shadcn/ui’s biggest idea wasn’t a component set — it was making you own the code instead of depending on it. cascivo keeps that idea and builds the rest of what you’d otherwise have to hand-roll on top of it.

item.title: Do you still need a component library with modern CSS?
item.description: @layer, @container, and :has() close real gaps that used to require a utility framework or JavaScript. Here’s what they replace, in code, and why "modern CSS is enough" and "you still want a component library" aren’t actually in tension.

item.title: Building an accessible command palette in React
item.description: A Cmd/Ctrl+K command palette looks simple until you get to focus trapping, screen-reader announcements, and keyboard navigation. Here’s what actually has to be right, and a working example.

item.title: Theming a React app with CSS custom properties, no build step
item.description: No config file, no build plugin, no CSS-in-JS runtime. A three-tier token system and a data-theme attribute — here’s how far you can get with plain CSS custom properties.

item.title: cascivo vs IBM Carbon: same enterprise bar, different architecture
item.description: IBM Carbon is a mature, genuinely accessible enterprise design system — it sets a real bar. cascivo tries to clear the same bar with a different architecture: copy-in ownership and native CSS instead of an npm dependency and SCSS.
